messages.content: What does my policy cover if a tree falls on my roof?

For a house resident: If a tree falls on your roof, your policy should cover the damage under the "weight of ice or snow" or possibly "windstorm" peril, but it's not explicitly mentioned as a named peril. However, since a tree falling on your roof could be considered a result of a windstorm or other covered event, you may be covered. It's essential to review your policy documents, specifically the declarations page, to understand the exact coverage limits and any endorsements that may apply. If you have an open-perils basis endorsement, you might have broader protection, but it's always best to check your individual policy for the most accurate information.

retrieved_context:
Section 4: What Is Not Covered (Exclusions)
This policy does not cover damage caused by flooding, earth movement, war, nuclear hazard, or
intentional acts by an insured person, as these are considered either catastrophic and uninsurable at a
standard premium, or outside the scope of accidental loss that homeowners insurance is designed to
address.
Gradual damage such as mold, wear and tear, insect or rodent damage, and lack of maintenance are
generally excluded, as these are considered preventable through routine upkeep rather than sudden,
accidental loss; a limited mold remediation benefit may apply only when mold results directly from a
separately covered water loss.
Business activities conducted from the home may require a separate business owner's policy, as this

This document is a general summary. The declarations page and any endorsements attached to your
specific policy take precedence over general descriptions in this document, and policyholders should
always refer to their individual policy documents for exact coverage limits, deductibles, and
endorsements in effect.
Named perils covered under the base policy include fire, lightning, windstorm, hail, explosion, riot,
aircraft or vehicle impact, smoke, vandalism, theft, falling objects, weight of ice or snow, and accidental
discharge or overflow of water or steam from a plumbing, heating, or air conditioning system.
Optional endorsements can extend coverage beyond the base named perils to an open-perils basis for

Optional endorsements can extend coverage beyond the base named perils to an open-perils basis for
the dwelling and other structures, meaning all causes of loss are covered except those specifically
excluded, which provides broader protection at a higher premium.
A separate liability umbrella policy may be purchased to extend personal liability coverage beyond the
limits provided in this base policy, which is recommended for homeowners with significant assets or
higher-than-average liability exposure such as a swimming pool or trampoline.

Section 2: Dwelling and Structure Coverage
Coverage A (Dwelling) insures the physical structure of your home against covered perils including fire,
windstorm, hail, and other named perils listed in the declarations, up to the dwelling coverage limit,
which should reflect the full replacement cost of rebuilding your home rather than its market value or
purchase price.
Coverage B (Other Structures) extends similar protection to structures not attached to the dwelling,
such as detached garages, sheds, and fences, typically at 10% of the dwelling coverage limit, though
this percentage can be increased with an endorsement for homeowners with significant outbuildings.
Flood and earthquake damage are not covered under this standard policy and require separate policies
